source.title: Rhythmic interactions between the mediodorsal thalamus and prefrontal cortex precede human visual perception

confidence_rationale: Human intracranial + MEG data showing MD thalamus at 7-8 Hz coupling with PFC at ~10-11 Hz is strong direct evidence for a characteristic thalamic frequency in the cognitive/deliberative loop. The frequency hierarchy (MD slower than PFC) aligns with ARC-023's prediction. However, only one thalamic nucleus-cortical target pair is tested, so extrapolation to a full three-loop frequency hierarchy requires inference.
failure_signatures: Cortical activity leads thalamic oscillations in this study, suggesting PFC drives MD rather than MD setting the pace -- this inverts ARC-023's framing of thalamic nuclei as rate-setters.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Mediodorsal thalamic low-frequency activity (7-8 Hz) and prefrontal low-frequency activity (~10-11 Hz) are rhythmically coupled, and this coupling predicts perceptual performance.
mapping.ree_translation: The MD-PFC oscillatory coupling at theta/alpha frequencies corresponds to the E3 deliberative heartbeat in ARC-023: MD maps to the thalamic relay for the E3 complex, and its 7-8 Hz rhythm represents the characteristic deliberation rate (slowest of the three loops).
mapping.mapping_caveat: The study shows PFC leading MD (cortex drives thalamus), whereas ARC-023 posits thalamic nuclei as rate-setters. The actual circuit may be a resonant loop rather than thalamus-to-cortex unidirectional pacing. Also, patient population (epilepsy) may not generalize.
mapping.source_context: Human patients with depth electrodes (epilepsy) + healthy controls with MEG; visual detection task.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Nature Communications, human intracranial + MEG, n=6 patients + 12 controls). Mapping fidelity moderate: MD-PFC coupling well-established but the study tests only one loop, and directionality (PFC leads) partially challenges ARC-023's thalamic rate-setting framing. Transfer risk low (human data, relevant brain regions).
